Functional connectivity was examined with network-based statistics (NBS) and graph theoretical metrics. During the naturalistic VR task, the ADHD group showed weaker task performance and stronger functional connectivity than the TD group. Group differences in functional connectivity were observed in widespread brain networks: particularly subcortical areas showed hyperconnectivity in ADHD. More restricted group differences in functional connectivity were observed during the Video Viewing, and there were no group differences in functional connectivity in the Resting State condition. These observations were consistent across NBS and graph theoretical analyses, although NBS revealed more pronounced group differences. Furthermore, during the VR task and Video Viewing, functional connectivity in TD controls was associated with task performance during the measurement, while Resting State activity in TD controls was correlated with ADHD symptoms rated over six months. We conclude that overt expression of the symptoms is correlated with aberrant brain connectivity in ADHD. Furthermore, naturalistic paradigms where clinical markers can be coupled with simultaneously occurring brain activity may further increase the interpretability of psychiatric neuroimaging findings.

title: A Cross-Lagged Prospective Network Analysis of Depression and Anxiety and Cognitive Functioning Components in Midlife Community Adult Women
abstract: Background: The scar theory proposes that heightened depression and anxiety precede and predict worse cognitive functioning outcomes, whereas the vulnerability model posits the opposite pathway. However, most investigations on this topic have been cross-sectional, which precludes causal inferences. Thus, our study used both contemporaneous and temporal cross-lagged panel network analysis to facilitate causal inferences in understanding the relations between psychopathology components and cognitive functioning. Methods: Racially-diverse midlife women (n = 3,302) participated in the Study of Women's Health Across the Nation across two time-points, spanning one year apart. Five psychopathology (anxiety symptoms, depressed mood, somatic symptoms, positive affect, interpersonal problems) and cognitive functioning nodes (working memory (WM), processing speed (PS), visual memory (VSM), auditory memory (ARM)) were assessed. Results: Contemporaneous networks yielded notable inverse between-node relations (edges) for interpersonal problems and reduced VSM and PS, and between depressed mood or anxiety symptoms and VSM, ARM, or PS. Moreover, nodes that had the highest likelihood to bridge psychopathology and cognitive functioning constructs were positive affect, anxiety symptoms, WM, and ARM. Temporal networks produced edges inconsistent with the vulnerability theory. Higher depressed mood and somatic symptoms and lower positive affect were related to reduced future PS, WM, and/or VSM. Likewise, greater interpersonal issues and anxiety symptoms were linked to poorer future ARM and WM. Also, positive affect had the strongest effect on future nodes. Conclusions: These results provide stronger support for the scar theory than the vulnerability theory.

title: Inflammation Mediates Longitudinal Relations Between Common Psychiatric Disorder Severity and Future Executive Function Impairment
abstract: Background: Scar models propose that elevated psychiatric disorder severity predisposes people to future decreased executive function (EF) through heightened inflammation. However, most prior research on this topic has been cross-sectional. We thus investigated if increased Time 1 (T1) common psychiatric disorder severity predicted Time 3 (T3) EF decrement via Time 2 (T2) inflammation in two unique samples. Methods: Community- dwelling adults participated in Study 1 (n = 614) and Study 2 (n = 945). Both studies measured T1 common psychiatric disorder severity (Composite International Diagnostic Interview–Short Form major depressive disorder, generalized anxiety disorder, and panic disorder severity scales), T2 inflammation (interleukin-6, C-reactive protein, and fibrinogen blood concentration), and T3 EF (Brief Test of Adult Cognition by Telephone). Structural equation modeling was conducted. Results: Greater T1 diagnostic severity predicted higher T2 inflammation (after 2 months in Study 1: Cohen’s d = 0.84; following 9 years in Study 2: d = 0.82). Moreover, higher T2 inflammation predicted lower T3 EF (after 18 months in Study 1: d = -1.30; following 9 years in Study 2: d = -1.18), with large effect sizes. Further, the mediation paths were significantly moderate-to-large in Study 1 (d = 0.76) and Study 2 (d = 0.69). Socio-demographic, lifestyle, medication use, and physical health variables did not moderate these mediation models. Conclusions: Inflammation may be a mechanism explaining the T1 common psychiatric disorder severity–T3 EF relation. Treatments that target inflammation and/or anxiety or depressive disorders may prevent some individuals from experiencing EF decline.

title: Mindfulness Enhances Cognitive Functioning: A Meta-Analysis of 111 Randomized Controlled Trials
abstract: Background: In theory, habitually exercising mindfulness skills can improve cognitive functioning abilities. However, no comprehensive quantitative reviews of the efficacy of MBIs on global and unique cognitive subdomains exist to date. Method: This meta-analysis examined the effects of MBIs on global cognition and 15 cognitive subdomains. Inclusion criteria: meditation naïve participants; participants randomly assigned to MBIs or no-treatment, waitlist, or active control; outcome measures included at least one behavioral cognitive assessment or subjective cognitive functioning measure; teaching mindfulness skills was the primary treatment focus. Exclusion criteria: inadequate data to calculate effect sizes; MBIs included only one session; control condition that contained any MBI form. Robust variance estimation and moderator analyses controlling for presence of reported treatment fidelity were conducted. Results: One-hundred-and-eleven RCTs (n = 9,538) met eligibility criteria. MBIs had small-to-moderate significant effects on global cognition, executive attention, WM accuracy, inhibition accuracy, shifting accuracy, sustained attention, and subjective cognitive functioning (vs. waitlist/no-treatment, average g = 0.257–0.643; vs. active controls, average g = 0.192–0.394). MBIs did not impact executive functioning (EF) latency indices, verbal fluency, processing speed, episodic memory, and cognitive error. Treatment effects were stronger for those with elevated psychiatric symptoms compared to healthy controls, and medical samples, studies with complete-case (vs. intention-to-treat) analysis, face-to-face (vs. self-guided) delivery, and use of non-standard MBI (vs. standard mindfulness-based stress reduction or mindfulness-based cognitive therapy). Conclusion: MBIs consistently yielded small-to-moderate yet practically meaningful effect sizes on global cognition and six cognitive subdomains that captured accuracy vs. latency-based indices of EF and sustained accuracy.

title: Mindfulness Enhances Cognitive Functioning: A Meta-Analysis of 95 Randomized Controlled Trials
abstract: Background: The past 30 years have witnessed growing scientific interest regarding the impact of mindfulness-based interventions (MBIs) on cognitive functions. Several theories propose that habitually exercising mindfulness skills can improve cognitive abilities, but no comprehensive quantitative reviews of the effect of MBIs on global and unique cognitive domains exist to date. Method: This systematic review thus examined the effects of MBI on global cognitive ability (GCA) and 16 specific cognitive domains. MBI randomized controlled trials (RCTs) that administered cognitive tests pre- and post-treatment were included. Open-trials, non-randomized MBIs, and case-control studies were excluded. Keywords included “mindful*,” AND “executive attention (EA),” OR “working memory (WM).” Robust variance estimation and moderator analyses were conducted. Results: Ninety-five RCTs (n = 7,408) met eligibility criteria. MBI (vs. waitlist or no-treatment) had small-to-moderate significant effects on GCA, WM accuracy, inhibition accuracy and latency, EA, sustained attention accuracy, processing speed, and subjective attentional control (SAC) (g = 0.24 – 0.52). Likewise, MBI (vs. active control) had small-to-moderate positive effects on GCA, orienting, EA, WM accuracy, sustained attention (indexed by intra-individual coefficient of variation), and SAC (average g = 0.17 – 0.41). Age, gender, study quality, treatment duration, publication year, retention, statistical analysis, and country, moderated some treatment effects. Publication bias analyses showed that reliable treatment effects were restricted to EA, WM accuracy, inhibition accuracy, sustained attention, and SAC, depending on the control group. Conclusion: MBIs confer notable neuropsychological benefits and dose-response effects on some specific (vs. global) cognitive domains. Limitations, theoretical, and applied implications are discussed. (Note: This paper has not been peer reviewed. Please do not copy or cite without author's permission.)

title: Mindfulness Enhances Cognitive Functioning: A Meta-Analysis of 111 Randomized Controlled Trials
abstract: Background: It has been theorized that habitually exercising mindfulness skills can improve cognitive functioning abilities, but no comprehensive quantitative reviews of the efficacy of MBIs on global and unique cognitive functioning domains exist to date. Method: This meta-analysis examined the effects of MBIs on global cognition and 16 specific accuracy and latency cognitive functioning domains. Inclusion criteria: participants were meditation naïve; studies randomly assigned participants to MBI or no-treatment, waitlist, or active control; outcome measures included at least one behavioral cognitive assessment or subjective attentional control measure; teaching mindfulness skills was the primary treatment focus. Exclusion criteria: data was inadequate to calculate effect sizes; MBI included only one session; control condition that contained any form of MBI. Robust variance estimation and moderator analyses were conducted. All analyses controlled for presence of reported treatment fidelity. Results: One-hundred-and-eleven RCTs (n = 7,946) met eligibility criteria. MBIs had small-to-moderate significant effects on global cognition, executive attention, WM accuracy, inhibition accuracy, shifting accuracy, sustained attention, and subjective attentional control (average g = 0.245–0.680 compared to waitlist or no-treatment; average g = 0.192–0.394 versus active controls). Overall, treatment effects were stronger for those with elevated medical or psychiatric symptoms compared to healthy controls, studies with completer (vs. intent-to-treat) analysis, in-person (vs. app-based) delivery, and use of standard mindfulness-based stress reduction or mindfulness-based cognitive therapy (vs. non-standard MBI). Conclusion: MBIs confer notable neuropsychological benefits on some, but not all cognitive functioning domains. Limitations, theoretical, and applied implications are discussed.

title: Changing social emotions through a socio-emotional learning intervention improves the willingness to help an out-group member: a comparative longitudinal lab-in-the-field study in  teenagers and young adults in four countries
abstract:
Although intergroup conflicts negatively affect children’s development worldwide, comparative
research on promoting peacebuilding behaviours in teenagers and young adults remains scarce. This longitudinal, comparative, lab-in-the-field study tested the impact of a socio-emotional learning intervention based on Humanitarian Principles (Humanity, Neutrality, Impartiality, Independence) and Values on social emotions (compassion, empathic distress, malicious joy) and on the willingness to help an in- or an out-group member (immigrant or local minority). 96 teenagers and young adults (mean [SD] age: 17.06 [3.56] years; female: 66) in Armenia, Belarus, The Gambia, and Russia were assigned to a three- or five-day intervention in Humanitarian Principles and Values or to a control group. Although there was no overall effect of the intervention on social emotions nor on the willingness to help an in- or an out-group member, an intervention-related increase in compassion and empathic distress predicted an intervention-related increase in the willingness to help an out-group member. We also observe differences between countries. This study highlights the importance of socio-emotional learning interventions in promoting prosocial behaviour in teenagers and young adults from different countries. In addition, this research contributes to the growing body of empirical and policy-oriented work on the implementation of socio-emotional learning and thus contributes to the Sustainable Development Goals 4 (Education) and 16 (Peace).

title: Mental Health, Pain Perception and Quality of Work Life Among Healthcare Professionals: A Latent Profile Analysis
abstract:
Objective: The objective was to estimate the perception of pain and its intensity among emergency department (ED) and intensive care units (ICU) professionals and to identify the relationship between pain and quality of work life (QWL). 
Design: A total of 145 ED and ICU professionals (senior physicians, interns, nurses, and nursing assistants) completed an online questionnaire assessing pain perception, through self-reported data, QWL (WRQoL), mental health (DASS-21) and burnout (MBI-HSS). 
Main results: 71.7% (n=104) experiencing work-related pain and 69.7% (n=76) consuming painkillers to alleviated it. Latent profile analyses identified 3 profiles of QWL: very poor (n=8), poor (n=50), acceptable (n=77). Compared to the acceptable QWL profile, very poor and poor profiles were associated with more frequently reported work-related pain (p=.012), painkiller consumption related to these pains (p=.031) and had higher scores of mental health disorders.
Conclusion: Our study reinforces the importance of directly addressing the improvement of their QWL to best prevent the development or exacerbation of mental health issues and pain.

title: Subtle Positive Facial Expression Recognition is Enhanced by Comedy Viewing: Moderating Roles of Empathy and Depression
abstract: Recognising subtle signs of positive emotion is essential for trust, cooperation, and social connection. However, everyday barriers—such as face masks—can hinder this ability. We tested whether a brief positive mood induction via comedy viewing could enhance recognition of subtle smiles, and whether this depended on mask-wearing and individual traits. Fifty-three adults (22 males, 31 females) completed a within-subject experiment, watching either a comedy performance or a neutral weather broadcast, then judging the valence of morphed facial expressions (10–30% intensity) shown with or without a mask. Comedy viewing significantly increased positive mood (POMS2-BF VA: p < .001, r = .43) and reduced negative mood (e.g., TA: p = .004, r = .69). Recognition accuracy for subtle positive expressions improved after comedy for unmasked faces (p = .0016, r = .09) but not for masked faces (p = .27), where performance remained at chance. Greater perspective-taking and lower depressive mood predicted greater improvement. Even participants with higher depressive symptoms showed measurable gains. These results demonstrate that a short, enjoyable activity—watching comedy—can sharpen attunement to others’ positive emotions, but only when key facial cues are visible, highlighting both the potential and limits of mood-based interventions for social communication.

title: Mapping Attachment: Proposing a Cognitive Network of Attachment Figures Unifying Attachment Anxiety, Avoidance, Functions, Strength, and Structure.
abstract: Theoretical understanding of attachment is essential to guide clinical practice. Internal Working Models (IWMs) of attachment are cognitive-affective structures accessible through language. They represent past interactions with attachment figures and enable individuals to formulate predictions regarding their behavior. Although formed in early childhood through repeated relational experiences, IWMs remain malleable in adulthood. While some authors conceptualize IWMs as an organized network, no integrated model currently exists. This narrative literature review aims to define a Cognitive Network of Attachment Figures (CNAF). This network is designed to integrate five key dimensions of attachment identified as complementary and assessed using three instruments: attachment anxiety and avoidance (ECR-RS), attachment functions and strength (WHOTO), and relational structure (HICLAS). The CNAF theory postulates that these dimensions and their content organize a connectionist network architecture in a distributed manner. The activation dynamics of this architecture allow it to generate transient representations by guiding information processing, directing social interactions, and directly or indirectly influencing social well-being. Furthermore, we posit that nonlinear dynamic systems theory provides a theoretical framework to identify emergent properties within the idiographic organization of data that influence social well-being, thereby offering a sufficiently complex understanding of the cognitive functioning of attachment.

title: Dissociable Roles of Reward Prediction Error in the Contrasting Mood Dynamics of Depression and Anxiety
abstract: Mood fluctuations, central to human experience, are profoundly influenced by reward prediction errors (RPE). Although depression and anxiety traditionally exhibit contrasting mood fluctuations, their interrelated nature has made it challenging to pinpoint their specific roles in RPE-induced mood variations. In this study, we employed a computational model of momentary mood within a gambling task, involving 2,043 participants across five experiments. Participants also completed a battery of questionnaires designed to allow us to dissociate anxiety- and depression-specific traits through bifactor modelling. Results showed that depression was associated with dampened mood fluctuations due to mood hyposensitivity to RPE. Importantly, this pattern was also found in patients with affective disorders. In contrast, anxiety correlated with heightened mood fluctuations stemming from mood hypersensitivity to RPE in non-clinical participants. Moreover, the shared depression/anxiety component was linked to lower affective baseline and greater risk aversion. Collectively, our results uncover computational dissociation of depression vs. anxiety using RPE-based mood modeling and present multi-dimensional computational signatures for these symptoms, with clinical relevance for management of mood disorders.

title: Presence of Dissociative Experiences in Chronic Illness: A Systematic Review
abstract: Phenomenological notions of embodiment can coincide or differ from clinical definitions of embodiment. For chronic illness, specifically those in which a sense that one’s life is altered going forward, research has established the need for individuals to have open and flexible mindsets to cope with pain and changes to one’s identity. The question we sought to ask was whether dissociative symptoms manifest in chronic illness presentations, and if so, how these symptoms may present given the possibilities that phenomenology offers for a broader definition of the experience. This systematic literature review followed the PRISMA format, sorting through several of the largest psychological and medical journal databases to identify eight articles that included mentions of dissociative symptoms in those with chronic illness. The full process for this review can be seen throughout this study. The themes we gathered included navigating chronic illness, coping, predisposition, experiential, somatization, and ways of relating. The discussion outlines how dissociative symptoms are not a significant feature to chronic illness, but present in a pre-symptom fashion that can be worsened to a distressing experience dependent on their mindset and coping strategies. Implications for treatment and future research are offered at the end.

title: Perception of Nature: A Cognitive Mechanism Linking Nature Exposure, Pro-Environmental Behaviour, and Well-Being
abstract: Spending time in nature has been associated with improved psychological well-being and greater pro-environmental behaviour. However, much less is known about whether time spent in nature is associated with changes in how individuals conceptualise what is considered ‘nature’. Here, guided by prototype theory, we conducted a cross-sectional survey study with the general public (N = 377, mean age = 29 years) to examine how perception of nature (operationalised as the breadth of environments individuals identify as nature) may function as a potential cognitive process linking time spent in nature, pro-environmental behaviour with three types of psychological well-being (happiness, depression, and anxiety), while accounting for age and sex. A broader perception of nature was directly associated with more time spent in nature. Perception of nature also consistently mediated the association between time spent in nature and pro-environmental behaviour across all models. Importantly, perception of nature was indirectly associated with happiness through pro-environmental behaviour, whereas it was directly associated with lower levels of depression. We also found that demographic differences in these measures. Older people spent more time in nature, had a broader perception of nature, and showed more pro-environmental behaviour than younger people. Males reported greater happiness and lower levels of depression and anxiety than females. Together, these findings are consistent with prototype theory, suggesting that the breadth of understanding of nature may expand through accumulated experience in different environmental settings. Having a broader perception of nature may represent a cognitive process linking time spent in nature and pro-environmental behaviour, which may partly explain associations with psychological well-being.

title: Towards Measuring the Social Sources of Self-Efficacy in Chronic Pain: An Exploratory Psychometric Study
abstract:
Background: Self-efficacy beliefs in chronic pain are shaped by both individual experiences of action and the social environments in which these experiences occur. Yet the conditions through which this sense of capability is formed, sustained, or eroded, including perceived support, vicarious learning, and the quality of the relational environment, have not been systematically targeted as a measurement object. 
Methods: This study reports the initial psychometric evaluation of a questionnaire designed to capture self-efficacy within the social environment of chronic pain. A 38-item instrument, the Pain Self-Efficacy Social Sources Scale (PSE3S), was created, covering both specific behavioural domains (physical activity, functional autonomy, pain communication) and social dimensions (perceived support from others, peer-based learning, and perceived quality of the social environment). Exploratory factor analysis using principal axis factoring with oblique rotation and parallel analysis was used to examine the factorial structure.
Results: A total of 229 adults from a general population sample completed the instrument. The analysis yielded a six-factor solution. Three factors corresponded to established selfefficacy domains: physical activity and leisure, autonomy, and communicating one’s pain experience. Two factors aligned with social sources of self-efficacy: social support and vicarious learning. A sixth factor captured perceived social belonging. Factors reflecting social dimensions were inter-correlated, whereas action-domain factors were largely independent. Internal consistency was good across both sections of the instrument (Mc- Donald’s ω = 0.98; ωh = 0.62). 
Conclusions: These results provide preliminary evidence that the social conditions shaping pain-related self-efficacy can be operationalised and empirically distinguished within a single instrument. The PSE3S targets the relational context through which these beliefs are formed, complementing rather than duplicating existing pain self-efficacy measures. Clinical validation in a chronic pain sample is the next step of the research program.

title: Cognitive offloading to artificial intelligence: a scoping review of performance, perceptions, and intentions
abstract:
Cognitive offloading, the practice of utilizing external tools to reduce mental demand, is
shifting from traditional memory aids to artificial intelligence (AI). While traditional offloading
is typically associated with lower-order cognitive tasks, AI facilitates the delegation of
complex, higher-order processes. Despite rapid adoption, the downstream consequences of
offloading to AI remain under-explored and evidence is mixed. Following PRISMA-SCR
guidelines, this scoping review synthesizes 20 empirical studies (2023–2025) to provide a
picture of this evolving field. We propose a categorization of the extant research into three
distinct domains: (1) intentions to offload, (2) effects on cognitive performance, and (3)
perceptions and beliefs. This framework serves as a tool for monitoring the rapid development
of cognitive offloading to AI and addressing the current difficulty of interpreting heterogeneous
approaches across the literature. Results reveal generally positive attitudes and high usage
intentions. While performance appears to increase during AI-assisted tasks, participants often
show no performance advantage compared to non-users once tools are removed. All studies
evaluating critical thinking report negative effects, suggesting a cognitive trade-off. To navigate
these complexities, we outline a future research agenda that emphasizes the need for
longitudinal investigations, as well as larger and more diverse participant samples.

title: Virtual Reality Gaming as a Non-Pharmacological Intervention for Pain Management During Medical Procedures: A Systematic Review
abstract:
Background
The virtual reality is an increasingly used technology for pain and anxiety management in patients. This study focuses on the effectiveness and safety of virtual reality games for these purposes.

Methods
The literature search was conducted until November 1, 2025. Four databases were used to identify relevant randomized controlled trial published between 2000 and 2025 concerning the use of virtual reality video games to reduce pain and anxiety or to improve stakeholder satisfaction (patients, caregivers and/or parents). The methodological quality of the included studies was assessed using the PEDro scales.

Results
A total of 963 articles was identified, of which 54 full-text meet the inclusion criteria. These studies addressed both intra-operative and post-operative pain. Considerable heterogeneity was observed across studies regarding patient populations (children or adults), game content (active, passive, or mixed; specific or non-specific), and types of medical procedures.

Conclusion
The reviewed studies indicate that virtual reality can be safely used during a wide range of medical procedures, more or less painful. Although data collected do not provide consistent and robust evidence for a significant reduction in drug consumption or anxiety level, the majority of studies reported positive results in terms of pain reduction through the use of virtual reality games, demonstrating the potential effectiveness of this technology as a therapeutic support during medical procedure. Moreover, the analysis of the heterogeneity of the results shows that studies reporting a significant reduction in pain are more common when the content is specifically designed for pain management and adapted for the target population. This observation points to the relevance of content specificity and user-centered design in maximizing therapeutic outcomes. Future research could further explore the differences between traditional games and games specifically designed for pain management, with the aim of optimizing their analgesic effect.

title: Different post-pandemic patterns in youth mental health and wellbeing after a global stressor: variation across domains and informants
abstract:
Objective. The COVID-19 pandemic was a major global stressor for children and adolescents, raising concerns about longer-term consequences for mental health and wellbeing. Many studies reported increased mental health problems during and shortly after the pandemic, but less is known about longer-term post-pandemic patterns across domains and informants.
Method. We used repeated cross-sectional data from Dutch children and adolescents aged 8-18 years in the Netherlands Twin Register (NTR, N = 39,178, 50% girls) and a representative general-population sample (N = 2,782, 48% girls). Parent- and child-reported mental health problems and wellbeing were assessed using pre-pandemic, pandemic, and post-pandemic data through 2025. Post-pandemic patterns were examined by comparing 2024/2025 outcomes with 2023 and pre-pandemic levels using pre-registered multilevel models.
Results. Parent-reported internalizing and externalizing problems in 2024/2025 did not differ from 2023 levels (B = -0.03 to 0.03), but remained higher than pre-pandemic levels (B = 0.28 to 0.31). Child-reported depressive symptoms were lower in 2025 than in 2023 (B =-2.31), anxiety did not differ (B =-0.75), and anger was higher (B =1.13). Compared with pre-pandemic levels, child-reported depression, anxiety, and anger were all higher in 2025 (p <.001). Wellbeing patterns were mixed across informants and samples (B =-1.05 to 0.10).
Conclusions. Up to 2025, youth mental health and wellbeing did not show a general return to pre-pandemic levels. Patterns differed across domains and informants, highlighting the importance of continued multi-informant monitoring.

title: “How Do You Feel?” Direct Valence Measurement Enables the Detection of Affect Shift Dynamics as Powerful Predictors of Psychological Well-Being
abstract: Valence is central to affective experience yet remains poorly operationalized in emotion science. Most research infers valence by categorizing discrete emotions as positive or negative, which may conflate distinct aspects of emotional experience. On the other hand, direct bipolar valence measurement—asking individuals "How do you feel?" on a continuum from negative to positive—allows participants to integrate contextual complexity into their affective reports. Critically, this approach also enables the detection and quantification of transitions between positive and negative affective states, opening new possibilities for studying affect dynamics. We tested whether metrics quantifying these directional transitions predict psychological well-being more effectively than traditional intensity-based measures. Across three ecological momentary assessment datasets with 345 participants and over 30,000 assessments, the positive to negative affect shift ratio—quantifying the propensity to transition from positive to negative affect—showed stronger predictive associations with well-being outcomes than means and standard deviations derived from the same valence scale. This pattern persisted across LASSO regression, hierarchical regression, and relative importance analyses, and remained robust even with only 3 daily assessments. These findings demonstrate that direct valence measurement offers both theoretical advantages by respecting participant-integrated experience and empirical advantages by enabling more predictive dynamics metrics, while remaining practical for clinical applications.

title: Does intergroup contact affect political attitudes and behaviours? - A longitudinal mediation analysis using the Chilean Longitudinal Social Survey (ELSOC)
abstract: Intergroup contact has been known to not only affect attitudes towards contacted and non-contacted outgroups, but also to affect people’s open and liberal thinking, which in turn affects a variety of human experiences, cognitions and behaviours outside the intergroup dimension (called cognitive liberalisation hypothesis or tertiary transfer effect, TTE). This manuscript explores one suggested TTE of intergroup contact affecting political attitudes and behaviours mediated via intergroup ideologies in a multi-verse approach combining several intergroup contact, intergroup ideologies and political attitudes and behaviours indicators. We used three waves of the Chilean Longitudinal Social Survey (ELSOC), a representative large-scale annual survey (N = 2,863), and random-intercept cross-lagged panel models as advanced analytical methods. We found numerous stable between-person associations between intergroup contact, intergroup ideologies and political attitudes and behaviours. We did not find consistent longitudinal evidence supporting a within-person process of intergroup contact affecting intergroup ideologies and in turn political attitudes and behaviours, as proposed by the cognitive liberalisation hypothesis. We contextualise our findings in the existing literature as well as the emerging TTE research and provide suggestions for future

title: Predictive models of mental health based on resting-state functional connectivity show limited generalizability across late childhood and early adolescence
abstract:
Late childhood and adolescence are marked by substantial reorganization of the brain’s
functional connectome and the emergence of many mental health problems. Although resting-
state functional connectivity (rsFC) has been widely used to predict mental health and related
behavioral phenotypes, it remains unclear whether such models generalize across development
or instead reflect age-specific brain-behavior mappings. Here, we tested the developmental
generalizability of whole-brain rsFC-based predictive models of mental health and cognitive
ability across three timepoints spanning ages 9-14 years in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive
Development Study. Using out-of-sample prediction and cross-timepoint model transfer, we
found that rsFC significantly predicted concurrent mental health and cognitive ability within
each age group, but models trained at one age often generalized poorly to data acquired 2-4
years earlier or later, particularly for mental health. In addition to this limited transfer, we
demonstrate developmental shifts in the associations between individual functional
connections and mental health and cognitive ability. These changes occurred alongside broader
reorganization of the functional connectome, including age-related differences in global
covariance structure and cortical network segregation. Together, our findings indicate that
brain-behavior mappings of mental health are highly dynamic across late childhood and
adolescence and may challenge the assumption that predictive models or candidate neural
biomarkers for mental health disorders derived at a single age remain valid across development.

title: Parental exhaustion in parents with ADHD: Contributing factors and implications for interventions
abstract:
Objective: Despite a growing body of research on adults with ADHD, little is known about how ADHD may impact parenting experiences. The current study addressed this gap, by examining parental exhaustion and its contributing factors in parents with ADHD. 

Method: Participants (n=118, 91.5% female) were parents who had been diagnosed (50%) or self-identified (50%) as having ADHD. Parents completed an online survey including measures of emotional exhaustion in the parental role and emotional distancing from one’s child. Demographic characteristics and measures of sensory sensitivity and perceived social and healthcare support were examined as potential contributing factors using linear regression models. 

Results: We observed four key findings. First, parents with ADHD reported high levels of emotional exhaustion, and to a lesser extent, emotional distancing. Second, emotional exhaustion and distancing showed few significant differences by demographic, diagnostic and family characteristics. Third, sensory sensitivity predicted both greater emotional exhaustion and distancing from one’s child. Fourth, parents’ feelings of isolation were associated with greater parental exhaustion, while reporting a strong support network and positive interactions with healthcare providers were protective. 

Conclusion: Experiences of parental exhaustion are common among parents with ADHD, particularly among parents who experience heightened sensory sensitivity and low social support. The findings suggest a greater need for neurodiversity-informed approaches to support for parents with ADHD.

title: Psychometric Validation of the Perceived Cognitive Assistance Scale: Exploratory and Confirmatory Evidence from LLM-Supported Decision-Making in Retail Trading
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as reasoning aids in complex decisions, but existing technology-adoption constructs do not directly capture decision-time cognitive support. This study validates Perceived Cognitive Assistance (PCA) - users' perceived expansion of cognitive capability during LLM-supported decision-making - in the demanding context of retail trading, because decisions are information-rich, uncertain, and consequential. Building on prior construct definition and content validation, we tested a nine-item PCA scale in two waves. Wave 1 used exploratory factor analysis in a pilot sample of LLM-using retail traders (N = 69). Wave 2 used preregistered confirmatory factor analysis and validity testing in an independent sample (N = 283). The one-factor model showed supportive fit on CFI (.966), TLI (.955), and SRMR (.049), with elevated RMSEA (.089). Reliability was strong (ω = .863–.883; α = .859–.881). PCA was adjacent to but empirically separable from perceived usefulness: HTMT was .845, the five-factor model outperformed a merged PCA-usefulness model, and ESEM reduced the inter-construct correlation to .775. PCA showed modest non-redundant association with complexity intentions, whereas the exploratory mediation pathway was not confirmed. Findings support PCA as a reliable measure of perceived cognitive scaffolding in LLM-supported decision-making.

title: A longitudinal qualitative study of facilitators and barriers to care-experienced children’s wellbeing during the transition to secondary school
abstract: While school transitions can be challenging for all children, those with experience of maltreatment, including care-experienced children, face additional complexities linked to poorer wellbeing outcomes. This study explored the barriers and facilitators influencing UK care-experienced children’s wellbeing during the transition from primary to secondary school. Twenty-six care-experienced children and 25 caregivers from England and Wales participated through the ReThink Programme. Semi-structured interviews were conducted with both groups before and after the transition, one year apart, and analysed using a framework-based thematic approach. Three overarching themes were identified: children’s sense of belonging, relationships and emotional support; the extent to which systemic support met individual needs; and children’s personal resources, including coping strategies and self-perceptions. Findings highlight dynamic interactions between relational, systemic and individual factors shaping wellbeing for maltreatment-exposed children across this period. While some experiences mirror those of the general population, distinct challenges were identified requiring targeted policy and practice responses.

title: Recommendations for Recruiting Clinically-Relevant Stratified Samples Across Race/Ethnicity, Gender, and Socioeconomic Status Using the Prolific Online Platform
abstract: Though online sampling methods have progressed, psychology literature continues to include primarily white, educated, upper-class samples. This work will outline the methodology of a stratified community study examining demographic differences in fear of happiness and depression among U.S. adults. Drawing on prior research suggesting variation in emotional processes across social identities, we implemented a fully crossed stratified sampling design to ensure equal representation across race/ethnicity (Black, Asian, Latine, White), gender (cisgender male, cisgender female), and socioeconomic status (SES). Eligible participants were 18-65 years old and residing in the U.S. SES categories were defined as ≤ $50,000 (low), $50,000 - $100,000 (middle), and ≥ $100,000 (high). A priori power analysis (G*Power) indicated a target of 42 participants per cell (N=1,008) to detect medium effects. Following the exclusion of 75 uncategorizable cases, the per-cell target was adjusted to 38, maintaining adequate power for medium-to-large effects. Participants were recruited in SES-based blocks via Prolific. Procedures to preserve stratification, confidentiality, and analytic rigor will be discussed, highlighting methodological considerations for equity-focused psychological research. These methods can ensure adequate power and representation when examining relationships between intersectional identities and psychological phenomena in online samples.
